evidence: HTTP-facing modules depend on concrete `ServerState` internals instead of service boundaries (`events.rs` uses `state.event_tx.subscribe()`; `records_list.rs` uses `state.persistent_store()` and Turso APIs directly; `dispatch_blocking.rs` extends `ServerState` and forwards internal dispatch calls).

Cross-module contracts are encoded as unshared string protocols across CLI/server/evolution flows (`insight_generator.rs` checks `SubmitSpec`, error substrings, and category literals; `storage.rs` maps status strings like `pending/running/passed`; `records_list.rs` exposes string filters and counts based on exact record-type casing).

`connect_postgres_store` couples connection establishment with two migration systems (`temper_store_postgres::migration::run_migrations` and `PostgresRecordStore::migrate`), binding startup order of independent subsystems.

Core server flow still mixes multi-tenant and legacy single-tenant behavior: `get_or_spawn_tenant_actor_with_fields` falls back to `self.transition_tables` in `crates/temper-server/src/state/entity_ops.rs`, while OData helpers in `crates/temper-server/src/odata/common.rs` fallback to legacy `entity_set_map`/`csdl_xml`.

Dispatch API is layered through pass-through wrappers (`dispatch_action` -> `dispatch_tenant_action` -> `dispatch_tenant_action_ext` -> `dispatch_tenant_action_core`) in `crates/temper-server/src/state/dispatch/actions.rs` with mostly default-argument forwarding.

Registry API has overlapping wrappers (`register_tenant*`, `try_register_tenant*`) and duplicated tenant compilation/hot-reload logic in `crates/temper-server/src/registry.rs`, increasing indirection without introducing distinct policy boundaries.

Large DST suites repeat the same simulation setup patterns (table builder helpers, `SimActorSystemConfig`, actor registration, lifecycle scripts) across `crates/temper-platform/tests/system_entity_dst.rs`, `reference-apps/ecommerce/tests/ecommerce_dst.rs`, and `reference-apps/oncall/tests/oncall_dst.rs`.

Verification orchestration is duplicated across runtime/bootstrap and CLI paths: `crates/temper-platform/src/bootstrap.rs` runs `VerificationCascade` directly during tenant bootstrap, while `crates/temper-cli/src/serve/mod.rs` and `crates/temper-cli/src/verify/mod.rs` each run separate cascade orchestration flows.

Reference-app DST tests depend directly on server-internal simulation handler (`temper_server::entity_actor::sim_handler::EntityActorHandler`) in `reference-apps/ecommerce/tests/ecommerce_dst.rs`, `reference-apps/oncall/tests/oncall_dst.rs`, and `crates/temper-server/tests/gmail_oauth_dst.rs`, creating cross-package boundary fragility.

Persistence concerns are concentrated into concrete Turso APIs (`crates/temper-store-turso/src/store.rs`) while callers in `crates/temper-cli/src/serve/mod.rs` and `crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/operations.rs` bind to many concrete methods, increasing coupling to one storage implementation.
crates/temper-evolution/src/lib.rs line 27 uses `#[allow(deprecated)]` specifically to keep re-exporting `store::RecordStore` at the crate root.
The same crate root simultaneously re-exports the newer record-chain-centric APIs (`chain`, `insight`, `records`, `PostgresRecordStore`), indicating old and new surfaces are still coexisting.
incomplete_migration.issues_preventing_higher_score: Deprecated public API is still intentionally exposed, so migration is not fully completed and downstream users can continue binding to legacy contracts.

evidence: holistic_context.structure only exposes directory_profiles in this packet (no root_files/coupling_matrix), but it still shows a flat overload at crates/temper-server/src/ with file_count=21 and mixed concerns (routing, authz, constraint checks, observability, webhooks, wasm).

Import-affinity mismatch: crates/temper-server/src/dispatch.rs is documented as an OData compatibility facade, yet it also owns AgentContext/extract_agent_context used broadly outside OData (state/dispatch/*.rs, reaction/dispatcher.rs, authz_helpers.rs, webhook_receiver.rs, tests).

Root straggler: crates/temper-server/src/constraint_engine.rs is referenced from OData paths (odata/bindings.rs, odata/write.rs, odata/common.rs), but remains at crate root rather than within odata/ boundary.

Duplicate boundary pattern: crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution.rs acts as a re-export shim over crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/*, creating a second entrypoint layer for the same concern.

Route modules under crates/temper-server/src/observe/ (agents.rs, entities.rs) embed backend query orchestration directly against ServerState stores, while storage abstractions live in crates/temper-observe, indicating blurred package layering.

crates/temper-cli/src/init/mod.rs test `test_init_creates_directory_structure` intentionally passes an absolute filesystem path as `name` and asserts Cargo package content includes that path, which validates behavior that is invalid for Cargo package naming.

The same init tests do not assert namespace validity under path-like input even though `to_namespace` transforms only `-`/`_`, allowing unresolved path separators to flow into generated namespace text.
No tests in assigned files validate cross-backend evolution-read compatibility (Postgres vs Turso) for Observe endpoints despite runtime branching and backend-specific behavior.

`crates/temper-runtime/src/scheduler/sim_actor_system.rs:229-289` (`step`) and `:362-448` (`run_random`) duplicate the same transition bookkeeping, invariant checks, and integration-callback scheduling with two separate control flows.

`crates/temper-runtime/src/scheduler/mod.rs:47-88` and `crates/temper-store-sim/src/lib.rs:66-99` both define near-identical `DeterministicRng` xorshift implementations with subtly different probability guards, indicating copy-based reuse instead of one deterministic core.

`crates/temper-platform/src/bootstrap.rs:72-133` and `:142-200` repeat the same parse→verify→register→mark-verified pipeline for system and agent specs, with only constants and tenant inputs changing.
`crates/temper-server/src/state/entity_ops.rs:205-243` and `:253-290` replicate authz conversion/telemetry/decision formatting logic in two sibling methods.

`crates/temper-server/src/registry.rs:296-445` combines cross-invariant parsing, entity-set mapping, hot-reload mutation, first-registration creation, webhook index rebuild, and verification reset in a single branch-heavy method.

SecurityContext is built directly from caller-controlled X-Temper-* headers, including principal kind (`crates/temper-authz/src/context.rs`:49-71), and comments explicitly note token validation is not implemented yet.

Evolution endpoints are inconsistent: `handle_update_feature_request` performs Cedar checks (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/operations.rs`:315-325) while sibling read/trigger endpoints (`handle_sentinel_check`, `handle_unmet_intents`, `handle_feature_requests`) have no auth gate (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/operations.rs`:168-301).

Tenant selection falls back silently when headers are missing (`crates/temper-server/src/odata/common.rs`:13-37), while entity history fallback ignores tenant by using `entity_type:entity_id` (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/entities.rs`:61,101-103), creating inconsistent tenant boundary behavior.

Multiple observe handlers log backend failures then return 200 with empty payloads, masking operational failures: agents (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/agents.rs`:109-119,165-176), evolution records/insights (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/records_list.rs`:66-80,117-127), feature requests (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/operations.rs`:293-300).

Within the same module, persistence failures are handled differently: `persist_alerts` returns 500 immediately (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/operations.rs`:77-79) while `persist_insights` only logs and continues (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/operations.rs`:129-131).

Core dispatch/state APIs still propagate plain `String` errors (`crates/temper-server/src/state/dispatch/actions.rs`:22,47,72,178; `crates/temper-server/src/state/entity_ops.rs`:211,253,297,309), while other crates expose structured error enums (`crates/temper-authz/src/error.rs`, `crates/temper-observe/src/error.rs`, `crates/temper-mcp/src/error.rs`).

Observe/evolution handlers expose mixed return contracts for sibling routes: `Json<_>` (`handle_unmet_intents`, `handle_feature_requests`), `Result<Json<_>, StatusCode>` (`handle_sentinel_check`), and `Result<Json<_>, (StatusCode, String)>` (`handle_update_feature_request`) in `crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/operations.rs`:168-363.

Observe read APIs differ in failure signaling for similar data-fetch behavior: some return `Result<..., StatusCode>` (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/specs.rs`:71-120), others always return `Json` and silently degrade to empty results (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/agents.rs`:81-177; `crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/records_list.rs`:19-128).

Tenant and identity inputs are not uniform across surface area: tenant can be implicit fallback (`crates/temper-server/src/odata/common.rs`:13-37), while agent identity may come from either `x-agent-id` or `x-temper-principal-id` (`crates/temper-server/src/dispatch.rs`:35-49), leading to inconsistent caller contract expectations.

findings.summary: ServerState is acting as a cross-cutting service locator, leaking storage/event/dispatch internals into endpoint modules.
evidence: `handle_events` directly subscribes to `state.event_tx` (`events.rs:46`) rather than using an event-stream service boundary.
`list_evolution_records` and `list_evolution_insights` call `state.persistent_store()` and then invoke Turso-specific methods in the handler (`records_list.rs:24-27`, `89-90`).
`dispatch_wasm_integrations_blocking` is implemented directly on `ServerState` and forwards a large internal call chain (`dispatch_blocking.rs:24-49`).
findings.suggestion: Introduce narrow domain services (e.g., `EventStreamReader`, `EvolutionRecordsReader`, `WasmDispatcher`) and inject them into handlers so modules depend on stable interfaces instead of `ServerState` internals.

findings.summary: Shared behavior across modules relies on brittle string literals instead of typed shared contracts.
evidence: `insight_generator.rs` drives core logic from action/error/status strings (`SubmitSpec`, `EntitySetNotFound`, `AuthzDenied`, `ActionNotFound`) (`79-84`, `283-284`, `313-317`, `393-397`).
`storage.rs` reconstructs verification semantics from status text (`pending`, `running`, fallback to `passed`) in `row_to_registry_status` (`167-176`).
`records_list.rs` exposes free-form `record_type`/`status` filters and hard-coded record-type counts (`11-14`, `56-63`), tightly coupling API shape to storage strings.
findings.suggestion: Define shared enums/value objects in a common crate for trajectory actions, error categories, record types, and verification statuses; serialize/deserialize only at boundaries.

findings.summary: Backend-specific branching and duplicated restoration flows indicate a missing backend-agnostic registry loading boundary.
evidence: `storage.rs` has two near-mirror restore paths (`load_registry_from_postgres` and `load_registry_from_turso`) with repeated tenant grouping/parsing/registration choreography (`212-294`, `296-360+`).
Despite that local abstraction effort, server handlers still query Turso directly from HTTP layer (`records_list.rs:24-27`, `89-90`) instead of consuming a shared backend-neutral read API.
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared `RegistrySnapshotReader`/`EvolutionRecordReader` trait with backend implementations and move restore/query orchestration behind that interface.

findings.summary: Store connection is tightly coupled to migration side effects across multiple subsystems, increasing startup blast radius.
evidence: `connect_postgres_store` both opens the pool and performs migrations for `temper_store_postgres` plus `temper_evolution` (`43-53`).

Read paths (e.g., evolution record handlers) assume those side effects already ran, creating implicit initialization-order coupling between bootstrap and serving (`records_list.rs` directly queries persistent data paths).
findings.suggestion: Split startup into explicit phases: `connect`, `migrate_store`, `migrate_evolution`, and `serve`; make handlers depend on a readiness gate rather than implicit side effects.

findings.summary: Legacy and multi-tenant paths are interwoven in core server abstractions.
evidence: `get_or_spawn_tenant_actor_with_fields` first reads `SpecRegistry` then falls back to legacy `self.transition_tables` in `entity_ops.rs`.
OData helpers (`resolve_entity_type`, `tenant_csdl_xml`, `tenant_entity_sets`) still fallback to legacy server fields when registry lookup misses in `odata/common.rs`.
`dispatch_action` is documented as legacy and forwards into tenant-based APIs, keeping compatibility semantics inside primary dispatch module.
findings.suggestion: Extract a dedicated legacy-compat adapter (or feature-gated shim) that normalizes legacy inputs into tenant-scoped registry data before entering core dispatch/entity paths, then remove fallback branches from core modules.

findings.summary: Public API variants add forwarding depth without meaningful behavior differences.
evidence: `registry.rs` exposes multiple `register_tenant*` and `try_register_tenant*` variants that mostly forward default `reactions`/`cross_invariants_source` values.
`dispatch/actions.rs` layers four dispatch entry points with mostly argument forwarding and default option packing.
`sim_dispatcher.rs` adds `register_entity` as a convenience wrapper that just constructs `EntityActorHandler` and calls `register_actor`, leaking concrete handler construction into dispatcher API.
findings.suggestion: Collapse overload chains into one primary API per module plus explicit option structs/builders; move convenience wrappers into extension traits or test-only helpers to keep core interfaces behavior-focused.

findings.summary: DST suites duplicate large simulation harness patterns instead of sharing a test DSL.
evidence: Each file redefines per-entity `TransitionTable` constructors from embedded IOA strings and repeatedly wires `SimActorSystem` + `EntityActorHandler`.
Scripted lifecycle tests across files repeat the same seed/config/register/step/assert pattern with only entity/action names changed.
The same deterministic simulation concerns (fault config, random exploration, reproducibility) are implemented independently in multiple suites.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared DST testkit (scenario DSL + entity registration helpers + reusable assertions) in one crate/module and migrate reference-app/platform/server DST suites to data-driven scenarios.

findings.summary: Verification orchestration is duplicated across runtime and CLI entry points.
evidence: `bootstrap.rs` imports `temper_verify::cascade::VerificationCascade` and runs per-spec cascade synchronously during tenant bootstrap.
`serve/mod.rs` spawns per-entity background verification with its own status/event lifecycle and hard-coded cascade params.
`verify/mod.rs` independently runs cascade loops and reporting, creating a third orchestration path.
findings.suggestion: Centralize verification orchestration in a single service/API (in `temper-verify` or a dedicated orchestration crate) and have bootstrap/serve/verify call that interface with context-specific policy flags instead of embedding separate flows.

findings.summary: Reference-app tests depend on server-internal simulation handler APIs.
evidence: Reference-app test files import `temper_server::entity_actor::sim_handler::EntityActorHandler` directly instead of a stable public testing API.
Tests also bind to low-level `TransitionTable` construction and server-specific handler composition, so internal server refactors can break external app tests.
The same pattern appears across multiple independent test suites, indicating a dependency boundary leak rather than an isolated choice.
findings.suggestion: Expose a stable simulation test API from `temper-verify`/`temper-runtime` (or a `testkit` crate) and migrate reference-app suites off `temper_server::entity_actor` internals.

findings.summary: Callers are tightly coupled to a monolithic Turso store surface.
evidence: `TursoEventStore` contains mixed responsibilities (events, specs, verification status, trajectories, wasm logs, policies, evolution records) in one concrete type.
`serve/mod.rs` calls multiple concrete Turso-specific methods for startup recovery and policy/spec state management.
`operations.rs` persists evolution records by directly depending on Turso store capabilities exposed via `state.persistent_store()` semantics.
findings.suggestion: Split persistence into bounded traits (event journal, spec metadata, evolution records, policy store) and depend on trait objects/interfaces in callers; keep Turso as one implementation behind those interfaces.

findings.summary: Crate root still exports deprecated `RecordStore`, prolonging legacy API usage.
evidence: `crates/temper-evolution/src/lib.rs` line 27 has `#[allow(deprecated)] pub use store::RecordStore;`, which bypasses deprecation warnings for consumers.

The crate root already promotes newer APIs (`records::*`, `PostgresRecordStore`, `chain`, `insight`), so keeping the deprecated re-export preserves a dual-path API surface instead of forcing migration.
findings.suggestion: Phase out the deprecated re-export by removing `pub use store::RecordStore` from the crate root, provide a compatibility feature flag or explicit migration alias module for a bounded period, and update internal/downstream imports to concrete non-deprecated store types before deleting the shim.

findings.summary: dispatch.rs combines legacy OData facade duties with cross-cutting agent identity context, creating a misleading module boundary.
evidence: crates/temper-server/src/dispatch.rs declares itself a compatibility facade for OData handlers, but also defines AgentContext and extract_agent_context.
Non-OData modules import AgentContext from dispatch (reaction/dispatcher.rs, state/dispatch/mod.rs, authz_helpers.rs, webhook_receiver.rs), so the file is no longer OData-scoped in practice.
This creates an import-affinity mismatch: identity/context ownership is spread under a module named and documented for OData dispatch.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: 1) Create `crates/temper-server/src/request_context/agent.rs` (or `http_context/agent.rs`) and move `AgentContext` + `extract_agent_context` there. 2) Update imports with `rg -l "crate::dispatch::AgentContext|crate::dispatch::extract_agent_context" crates/temper-server/src crates/temper-server/tests | xargs sed`-style targeted edits, keeping `dispatch.rs` OData-only. 3) Keep temporary re-exports in `dispatch.rs` for one release if needed, then remove. 4) Validate with `cargo check -p temper-server` and `cargo test -p temper-server`.

findings.summary: constraint_engine.rs sits in the crowded crate root even though its usage is OData-write specific.
evidence: directory_profiles reports `crates/temper-server/src/` file_count=21, indicating a broad flat root.
Constraint engine APIs are imported by OData modules (bindings.rs, write.rs, common.rs) rather than widely across unrelated server domains.
Keeping this policy/checking unit at root obscures that it is part of OData write-path enforcement.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: 1) Move `constraint_engine.rs` to `crates/temper-server/src/odata/constraints.rs`. 2) Wire through `crates/temper-server/src/odata/mod.rs` (`pub(crate) mod constraints;` and selective re-exports if external callsites remain). 3) Update imports via `rg -l "constraint_engine::" crates/temper-server/src | xargs` replacements to `odata::constraints::`. 4) Validate with `cargo check -p temper-server` and focused tests `cargo test -p temper-server odata`.

findings.summary: observe/evolution.rs is a shim over observe/evolution/*, adding an avoidable extra package layer for the same domain.
evidence: crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution.rs only declares submodules and re-exports handlers from `observe/evolution/*`.
Router wiring in observe/mod.rs calls through this shim, so navigation requires understanding both a facade file and the real implementation directory.
This repeats the same compatibility-layer pattern seen in dispatch.rs and contributes to boundary ambiguity.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: 1) Replace `crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution.rs` with `crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/mod.rs` and keep handlers directly declared there. 2) Update `observe/mod.rs` references if needed to avoid extra re-export hops. 3) Remove obsolete file-level facade docs/comments to reduce ambiguity. 4) Validate with `cargo check -p temper-server` and `cargo test -p temper-server observe`.

findings.summary: observe route modules mix HTTP concerns with storage-query orchestration, weakening directory boundaries between transport and data access.
evidence: agents.rs directly drives persistent-store queries (`query_agent_summaries`, `query_trajectories_by_agent`) and response shaping in the same handler module.
entities.rs performs actor-registry reads, event store fallback logic, and SSE emission in one route file.
At the same time, `temper-observe` already hosts observability storage abstractions/adapters, so server `observe/` currently carries mixed transport + backend orchestration responsibilities.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: 1) Introduce `crates/temper-server/src/observe/service/` (e.g., `agent_audit.rs`, `entity_history.rs`) and move Turso/event-store orchestration there. 2) Keep `observe/agents.rs` and `observe/entities.rs` as thin axum adapters calling service functions. 3) Move shared observability DTOs that are transport-agnostic into `temper-observe` only where cross-crate reuse is proven, then update imports. 4) Validate with `cargo check -p temper-server -p temper-observe` and `cargo test -p temper-server`.

findings.summary: Observe evolution APIs are Turso-only despite documented Postgres/Turso backend contract
evidence: README.md states persistence is `Postgres or Turso/libSQL` and lists Observe dashboard as implemented.
records_list.rs comments and implementation explicitly query Turso (`state.persistent_store()`) and return an empty successful response when Turso is unavailable or query fails.
storage.rs sets up Postgres evolution persistence (`PostgresRecordStore::migrate`) but Observe read handlers do not expose that backend.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a backend-neutral evolution read contract in server state (for example `EvolutionReadStore`), implement Postgres and Turso adapters, and make `list_evolution_records`/`list_evolution_insights` call that interface. If the configured backend is unavailable, return a surfaced error (5xx) instead of silent empty data. Add integration tests for both backend modes (Turso-only and Postgres-only) covering successful and failure paths.

findings.summary: Insight generation uses conflicting resolution rules for the same unmet-intent concept
evidence: `generate_insights` marks unresolved `EntitySetNotFound` as resolved if any `SubmitSpec` action exists for the entity type, without checking success status or ordering.
`generate_unmet_intents` marks resolution only when `SubmitSpec` is successful and stores resolution timestamp.
README positions Evolution Engine as a coherent O-P-A-D-I chain; divergent resolution semantics produce contradictory downstream records.
findings.suggestion: Extract one shared resolver for unmet-intent lifecycle semantics (status, success requirement, and time-ordering) and make both generators consume it. Define explicit contract tests with sequences that include failed `SubmitSpec`, out-of-order timestamps, and repeated submissions so both outputs stay consistent.

findings.summary: Init tests validate path-as-name behavior that masks invalid scaffold contracts
evidence: `run(name)` uses one input for directory path, Cargo package name template replacement, and namespace derivation.
Test `test_init_creates_directory_structure` passes an absolute path as `name` and asserts `Cargo.toml` contains that absolute path, endorsing a non-portable package-name contract.
`to_namespace` only splits on `-` and `_`, so path separators can pass through under path-like input while tests only check for `Namespace=\"Temper.` prefix.
findings.suggestion: Separate scaffold API inputs into `target_dir` and validated `project_name` (or derive `project_name` from basename), reject invalid Cargo/package and namespace characters, and update tests to assert normalized basename output. Add table-driven tests for relative name, absolute path target with explicit project name, and invalid name rejection.

findings.summary: SimActorSystem keeps two independent transition-processing paths that must stay behaviorally identical.
evidence: `step` handles status snapshotting, action counting, transition recording, invariant checks, and integration callback scheduling at `sim_actor_system.rs:240-278`.
`run_random` re-implements the same sequence for delivered messages at `sim_actor_system.rs:401-433`, with separate failure paths and side-effect ordering.
Both flows rely on shared scheduler/fault semantics from `scheduler/mod.rs`, but correctness now depends on manually maintaining duplicated logic.
findings.suggestion: Extract a single internal `process_transition(actor_id, action, payload, tick_source)` routine used by both scripted and random modes, so invariant checks, counters, and callback scheduling cannot drift.

findings.summary: Deterministic RNG logic is duplicated across crates with diverging edge-case behavior.
evidence: Both files define a local `DeterministicRng` with the same xorshift implementation (`scheduler/mod.rs:47-88`, `store-sim/lib.rs:66-99`).
`chance` behavior differs: scheduler uses threshold compare directly (`mod.rs:79-82`), while store-sim short-circuits for `<=0.0` and `>=1.0` (`lib.rs:89-97`).
This creates two randomness contracts for the same deterministic-testing domain, increasing risk of inconsistent fault-injection expectations.
findings.suggestion: Move RNG into one shared deterministic utility (likely `temper-runtime`) and have both scheduler and sim store consume the same implementation and probability contract.

findings.summary: Bootstrap logic is copy-pasted for system and agent specs instead of being parameterized.
evidence: `bootstrap_system_tenant` and `bootstrap_agent_specs` each perform the same four stages: parse all specs, run per-spec cascade, parse CSDL, register and write `VerificationStatus::Completed` (`bootstrap.rs:72-133`, `:142-200`).
Both functions manually build the same `EntityVerificationResult` shape and per-entity registry update loop (`bootstrap.rs:110-126`, `:176-192`).
This duplicates policy for how verified bootstrap entities are represented in registry state, making it easy for one bootstrap path to diverge from the other.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a generic `bootstrap_spec_bundle(state, tenant_id, csdl_xml, specs)` helper returning structured outcomes; keep only bundle constants and tenant selection in the public wrappers.

findings.summary: ServerState has two authorization entrypoints with duplicated decision and telemetry choreography.
evidence: `authorize` and `authorize_with_context` duplicate conversion from `BTreeMap` to `HashMap`, call `authorize_or_bypass`, build wide-event telemetry, and map `AuthzDecision` to `Result<(), String>` (`entity_ops.rs:205-243` and `:253-290`).
The only material difference is how `SecurityContext` is obtained (`from_headers` vs prebuilt), but both methods inline the same post-decision handling.
Because `AuthzEngine` already encapsulates decision logic (`engine.rs:217-229`), this extra duplicated wrapper policy is a maintenance hotspot.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate shared logic into a single private `authorize_internal(&SecurityContext, ...)` and keep the public methods as thin context adapters.

findings.summary: Tenant registration function mixes too many responsibilities and duplicates entity-build logic across branches.
evidence: `try_register_tenant_with_reactions_and_constraints` performs parsing, graph compilation, entity-set indexing, hot-reload updates, first-time insertion, route rebuilding, and verification resets in one method (`registry.rs:296-445`).
The hot-reload and first-registration branches both repeat IOA parse + table build + integrations extraction (`registry.rs:344-377` and `:400-418`).
Bootstrap callers rely on this broad method as a central write path (`bootstrap.rs:102-108`, `:173-175`), increasing blast radius for any registration change.
findings.suggestion: Split registration into composable phases (`parse_inputs`, `build_entity_specs`, `apply_to_existing`, `create_new_tenant`, `refresh_indexes`) and share one entity-spec builder between branches.

findings.summary: Authorization identity is derived from unverified client headers, allowing caller-selected principal kind/ID at policy boundaries.
evidence: `SecurityContext::from_headers` directly maps `x-temper-principal-id` and `x-temper-principal-kind` into the principal (`crates/temper-authz/src/context.rs`:51-71).
Auth helper forwards extracted headers without trust verification (`crates/temper-server/src/authz_helpers.rs`:17-40).
Authorization decisions and admin bypasses in evolution update are keyed off that context (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/operations.rs`:315-325).
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single authenticated identity middleware that validates signed tokens/session and constructs `SecurityContext` from trusted claims only; reject or ignore caller-provided principal-kind headers unless explicitly allowed in a development mode.

findings.summary: Sibling observe/evolution endpoints apply Cedar checks inconsistently, leaving sensitive reads/triggers unauthenticated while patch is gated.
evidence: `handle_update_feature_request` performs authz (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/operations.rs`:315-325), but `handle_sentinel_check`, `handle_unmet_intents`, and `handle_feature_requests` have no equivalent check (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/operations.rs`:168-301).
Module export/reuse exposes these operations together as one API surface (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution.rs`:8-14).
Agent audit endpoints (`list_agents`, `get_agent_history`) also expose tenant/agent activity without auth gating (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/agents.rs`:81-177).
findings.suggestion: Apply a uniform authorization policy per observe route class (read telemetry, trigger jobs, mutate state) via shared guard helpers or router-level middleware, and require explicit allow-listing for intentionally public endpoints.

findings.summary: Tenant scoping is inconsistently applied: tenant is extracted but not used in history-store fallback path, enabling implicit default-tenant reads.
evidence: Tenant extraction silently falls back when header is missing (`crates/temper-server/src/odata/common.rs`:13-37).
`get_entity_history` computes tenant-aware actor key, but store fallback uses `format!("{entity_type}:{entity_id}")` and drops tenant (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/entities.rs`:61-65,101-103).

Store read path parses `persistence_id` into tenant/entity components and queries by tenant (`crates/temper-store-postgres/src/store.rs`:125-137), so missing tenant in caller ID changes scope semantics.
findings.suggestion: Make tenant mandatory for persisted history lookups (`tenant:entity_type:entity_id`), and return explicit 400/401 when tenant context is absent instead of fallbacking to implicit/default tenant selection.

findings.summary: Observe endpoints convert backend errors into successful empty responses, hiding outages and corrupting client semantics.
evidence: On Turso query errors, handlers log warnings and return empty success payloads in agents endpoints (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/agents.rs`:109-119,165-176).
Same pattern in evolution records/insights list endpoints (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/records_list.rs`:66-80,117-127).
Feature request listing also degrades to `{ feature_requests: [], total: 0 }` on query failure (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/operations.rs`:293-300).
findings.suggestion: Return explicit typed failures (e.g., 503 for unavailable backend, 500 for query failures) and reserve empty arrays for true zero-result queries; keep logs but stop collapsing operational errors into success responses.

findings.summary: Closely related observe endpoints expose incompatible response/error shapes, forcing route-specific client error handling.
evidence: Evolution operations mix `Json<_>`, `Result<Json<_>, StatusCode>`, and `Result<Json<_>, (StatusCode, String)>` in the same module (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/operations.rs`:168-363).

Specs endpoint uses status-code errors (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/specs.rs`:71-120), while agents endpoints never surface status errors and always return `Json` (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/agents.rs`:81-177).
This creates divergent wire contracts for conceptually similar read/report endpoints under `/observe`.
findings.suggestion: Define one observe API error envelope and one handler contract pattern (e.g., `Result<Json<T>, ApiError>`), then migrate all observe modules to it so status codes and payload schemas are predictable.
